annotations.label: Cross product (binary operation)

anchor.snippet: the cross product or vector product (occasionally directed area product

annotations.note: `crossProduct` is the bilinear cross product on `Fin 3 → R` for a commutative ring `R`.

annotations.label: Magnitude equals parallelogram area

anchor.snippet: The magnitude of the cross product equals the area of a parallelogram with the vectors for sides

annotations.note: Proves ‖a ⨯₃ b‖ = ‖a‖·‖b‖·sin(angle a b), which is the parallelogram-area formula.

annotations.label: Anticommutativity and distributivity

anchor.snippet: The cross product is anticommutative

annotations.note: `cross_anticomm` gives anticommutativity; distributivity is built into `crossProduct` as a `LinearMap`.

annotations.label: Pseudovector property

anchor.snippet: Therefore, the cross product is a pseudovector

annotations.note: No formalization of the pseudovector/improper-rotation behaviour of the cross product was found.

annotations.label: Binary cross product exists only in 3 and 7 dimensions

anchor.snippet: But if the product is limited to non-trivial binary products with vector results, it exists only in three and seven dimensions.

annotations.note: No Mathlib result classifying dimensions admitting a nontrivial bilinear vector-valued cross product.

annotations.label: Cross product defined

anchor.snippet: The cross product a × b is defined as a vector c that is perpendicular (orthogonal) to both a and b

annotations.note: `crossProduct` is defined directly; orthogonality to both factors is `dot_self_cross` and `dot_cross_self`.

annotations.label: Cross product of parallel vectors is zero

anchor.snippet: If the vectors a and b are parallel

annotations.note: `crossProduct v w ≠ 0 ↔ LinearIndependent F ![v, w]` characterises vanishing as parallelism over a field.

annotations.label: Right-hand rule direction

anchor.snippet: Conventionally, it is given by the right-hand rule

annotations.note: No explicit right-hand-rule/orientation statement for the cross product is present.

annotations.label: Basis vector cross product equalities

anchor.snippet: is a positively oriented orthonormal basis , the basis vectors satisfy the following equalities

annotations.note: The relations e₁×e₂=e₃ etc. follow immediately from `cross_apply` but no named lemmas state them.

annotations.label: Self cross product is zero

anchor.snippet: The anticommutativity of the cross product (and the obvious lack of linear independence) also implies that

annotations.note: `cross_self : v ⨯₃ v = 0` matches exactly.

annotations.label: Component formula via distributivity

anchor.snippet: meaning that the three scalar components of the resulting vector

annotations.note: `cross_apply` gives the explicit component formula `![a₁b₂−a₂b₁, …]`.

annotations.label: Determinant formula for cross product

anchor.snippet: The cross product can also be expressed as the formal determinant

annotations.note: The scalar triple product is identified with a determinant, but the formal-determinant expansion of the cross product itself is not stated as a lemma.

annotations.label: Tensorial formula via Levi-Civita tensor

anchor.snippet: is given by the tensorial formula

annotations.note: No Levi-Civita tensor or tensorial cross-product formula was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pseudo-tensorial formula in orthonormal basis

anchor.snippet: In an orthonormal basis having the same orientation as the space

annotations.note: No formalization of the orthonormal-basis Levi-Civita formula was found.

annotations.label: Magnitude equals parallelogram area (geometric)

anchor.snippet: The magnitude of the cross product can be interpreted as the positive area of the parallelogram

annotations.note: ‖a ⨯₃ b‖ = ‖a‖·‖b‖·sin(angle a b) is the parallelogram-area identity.

annotations.label: Parallelepiped volume via scalar triple product

anchor.snippet: Indeed, one can also compute the volume V of a parallelepiped

annotations.note: `u ⬝ᵥ v ⨯₃ w = Matrix.det ![u, v, w]` is the algebraic content of the parallelepiped-volume formula.

annotations.label: Zero cross product characterization

anchor.snippet: If the cross product of two vectors is the zero vector

annotations.note: Vanishing iff linear dependence is proved over a field.

annotations.label: Anticommutativity, distributivity, scalar compatibility

anchor.snippet: The cross product is anticommutative ,

annotations.note: `cross_anticomm` plus the bilinear `crossProduct : … →ₗ[R] … →ₗ[R] …` give all three properties.

annotations.label: Jacobi identity for cross product

anchor.snippet: It is not associative , but satisfies the Jacobi identity

annotations.note: `jacobi_cross` is the named Jacobi identity for the cross product.

annotations.label: Failure of cancellation law

anchor.snippet: The cross product does not obey the cancellation law

annotations.note: No explicit non-cancellation statement found.

annotations.label: Rotation invariance of cross product

anchor.snippet: From the geometrical definition, the cross product is invariant under proper rotations

annotations.note: No SO(3)-invariance lemma for the cross product is present.

annotations.label: Cross product under matrix transformation

anchor.snippet: More generally, the cross product obeys the following identity under matrix transformations

annotations.note: The identity (Ma)×(Mb) = (det M)·M⁻ᵀ(a×b) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Product rule for cross product differentiation

anchor.snippet: The product rule of differential calculus applies to any bilinear operation

annotations.note: Generic bilinear product rules exist in Mathlib's calculus library but no dedicated lemma for `d(a × b)/dt` was found.

annotations.label: Scalar triple product

anchor.snippet: The scalar triple product of three vectors is defined as

annotations.note: Treated via `u ⬝ᵥ v ⨯₃ w` with `triple_product_eq_det` and `triple_product_permutation`.

annotations.label: Vector triple product (BAC-CAB)

anchor.snippet: The vector triple product is the cross product of a vector with the result of another cross product

annotations.note: `cross_cross_eq_smul_sub_smul'` proves u ⨯₃ (v ⨯₃ w) = (u·w)v − (v·u)w, the BAC-CAB rule.

annotations.label: Gram determinant relation

anchor.snippet: The right-hand side is the Gram determinant of a and b

annotations.note: `cross_dot_cross` specialised to u=w, v=x yields ‖a×b‖² = (a·a)(b·b) − (a·b)².

annotations.label: Cross product inverse solvability

anchor.snippet: Given two vectors a and c with a ≠ 0 , the equation a × b = c admits solutions

annotations.note: No solvability/inverse statement for a×b = c was found.

annotations.label: Lagrange's identity

anchor.snippet: can be compared with another relation involving the right-hand side, namely Lagrange's identity

annotations.note: `cross_dot_cross` with u=w, v=x is Lagrange's identity ‖a×b‖² = ‖a‖²‖b‖² − (a·b)².

annotations.label: Binet–Cauchy identity

anchor.snippet: which is the three dimensional case of the Binet–Cauchy identity

annotations.note: `cross_dot_cross` is documented as the scalar quadruple product, the 3-D Binet–Cauchy identity.

annotations.label: Cross product as skew-symmetric matrix product

anchor.snippet: The vector cross product also can be expressed as the product of a skew-symmetric matrix and a vector

annotations.note: No `[a]_× · b = a × b` formalization in Mathlib was found.

annotations.label: Isomorphism between R^3 and so(3)

anchor.snippet: the Lie algebra R 3 with cross product is isomorphic to the Lie algebra so(3)

annotations.note: `Cross.lieRing` makes (Fin 3 → R, ⨯₃) a Lie ring, but no isomorphism with so(3) is constructed.

annotations.label: Orthogonal projection matrix formula

anchor.snippet: The orthogonal projection matrix of a vector

annotations.note: No projection-via-skew-symmetric-square identity tied to the cross product was found.

annotations.label: Cross product via Levi-Civita tensor

anchor.snippet: The cross product can alternatively be defined in terms of the Levi-Civita tensor

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Levi-Civita symbol/tensor and no such definition of the cross product.

annotations.label: xyzzy mnemonic

anchor.snippet: The word "xyzzy" can be used to remember the definition of the cross product.

annotations.note: Mnemonic device, not a mathematical statement to formalize.

annotations.label: Distance between skew lines

anchor.snippet: The cross product appears in the calculation of the distance of two skew lines

annotations.note: No skew-line distance formula via cross product was located.

annotations.label: Sign of acute angle from three points

anchor.snippet: the cross product is used to determine the sign of the acute angle defined by three points

annotations.note: No formalization of this computational-geometry use of the cross product was found.

annotations.label: Angular momentum

anchor.snippet: The angular momentum L of a particle about a given origin is defined as:

annotations.note: No angular momentum definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Moment of force (torque)

anchor.snippet: the moment M of a force F B applied at point B around point A is given as:

annotations.note: No definition of torque/moment of force in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rigid body velocity relation

anchor.snippet: Two points P and Q on a rigid body can be related by:

annotations.note: Rigid-body kinematics is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorentz force

anchor.snippet: The cross product is used to describe the Lorentz force experienced by a moving electric charge

annotations.note: No Lorentz force definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cross product as Hodge star of bivector

anchor.snippet: The cross product is then obtained by taking the Hodge star of the bivector

annotations.note: Mathlib has exterior algebra but no Hodge star and no such identification with the cross product.

annotations.label: Heisenberg algebra

anchor.snippet: the Heisenberg algebra gives another Lie algebra structure

annotations.note: No Heisenberg algebra appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cross product via quaternions

anchor.snippet: the cross product of two vectors can be obtained by taking their product as quaternions and deleting the real part of the result

annotations.note: Mathlib defines quaternions but does not relate their product to the 3-D cross product.

annotations.label: Hurwitz nonexistence for other dimensions

anchor.snippet: The nonexistence of nontrivial vector-valued cross products of two vectors in other dimensions is related to the result from Hurwitz's theorem

annotations.note: Hurwitz's normed-division-algebra theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Exterior product generalization

anchor.snippet: The Hodge dual of the exterior product yields an

annotations.note: Mathlib has the exterior algebra and exterior powers but no Hodge dual realising the cross product.

annotations.label: External product

anchor.snippet: This generalization is called external product

annotations.note: No 'external product' generalisation of the cross product was located.

annotations.label: Commutator product

anchor.snippet: The commutator product is defined for 2-vectors

annotations.note: Geometric-algebra commutator product on bivectors is not formalized.

annotations.label: Generalized n-ary cross product

anchor.snippet: given

annotations.note: No n-ary cross product on n−1 vectors in Rⁿ was found.

annotations.label: Coordinate formula for n-ary cross product

anchor.snippet: In coordinates, one can give a formula for this

annotations.note: No determinant/minor coordinate formula for the n-ary cross product is present.

annotations.label: Filippov identity / n-Lie algebra structure

anchor.snippet: satisfies the Filippov identity

annotations.note: No Filippov identity or n-Lie algebra structure in Mathlib.
